prompt:
Security-compliance ticket. Quarterly audit wants a complete inventory of every KV v2 secret path across our Vault Enterprise namespaces, and the current 'tool' is a PowerShell one-liner that GETs a few hard-coded paths, treats every 404 as 'no secrets' (Vault 404s are NOT that simple — check the docs), and doesn't know child namespaces exist. Build me a real C# inventory client for this repo (test project VaultNamespaceList.csproj is already set up; add your implementation as source files alongside it — I'd expect the HTTP/list layer, the exception type, and the walker/inventory logic in separate files).

Start with research, before changing code: use WebSearch and WebFetch to read the CURRENT official Vault docs on developer.hashicorp.com — the HTTP API overview (how list operations work at the HTTP level, INCLUDING the documented alternative for HTTP stacks that can't send the special verb, and what a 404 with an empty errors array means), the KV v2 API reference (metadata listing, folder convention), the /sys/namespaces API page, and the Vault Enterprise namespaces concept page (how X-Vault-Namespace composes with relative child paths). Only first-party official pages count as sources. The pinned wire contract is docs/contract.json with provenance in docs/official_sources.json; both are protected. If the live official docs contradict them, report the conflict explicitly in your summary and leave fixtures and tests untouched. Cite the exact official URLs you actually read in your final message.

Public surface the tests compile against (namespace-less top-level types are fine):

- VaultListClient(HttpClient http, Uri baseAddress, string token, string? vaultNamespace = null, bool useListFallback = false) — every request authenticates with the documented token header (never in the URL), targets the configured namespace via the documented header exactly when one is set (root = header absent), and lists using the documented LIST verb — or, when useListFallback is true, the documented GET fallback.
- Task<IReadOnlyList<string>> ListKeysAsync(string apiPath, CancellationToken ct = default) — apiPath like "secret/metadata/app"; returns data.keys in response order. The empty-list response shape (read the docs for exactly what Vault sends) yields an empty list; any non-2xx with a NON-empty errors array throws VaultApiException with int StatusCode and IReadOnlyList<string> Errors, message carrying the status.
- Task<IReadOnlyList<VaultNamespaceInfo>> ListNamespacesAsync(...) — the Enterprise namespaces list endpoint; parse key_info into VaultNamespaceInfo(string Path, string Id) records, ordinally ordered by Path; the empty-list shape means no children.
- Task<IReadOnlyList<string>> WalkSecretPathsAsync(string mount, ...) — enumerate the whole KV v2 tree under the mount by listing metadata paths: descend folder keys (mind the documented folder convention — and note a name can be both a leaf and a folder), never list leaves, treat an empty-list 404 mid-walk as an empty folder, and return mount-relative secret paths ordinally sorted.
- Task<IReadOnlyList<VaultSecretRef>> InventoryAsync(string mount, bool includeChildNamespaces, ...) — VaultSecretRef(string Namespace, string Path). Walks the configured namespace, and when includeChildNamespaces is true recurses into every descendant namespace (children, their children, ...). Namespace targeting must be consistent: child paths from the namespaces endpoint are RELATIVE, so compose them onto the configured namespace for the header (eng/ + team-a/ => eng/team-a/); a root client uses "" as the Namespace value in results. Order entries ordinally by Namespace then Path.

Acceptance is VaultInventoryTests.cs — it runs a loopback fake Vault over raw TCP (so your LIST verb reaches it exactly as sent); no real Vault, no real credentials. Run: dotnet test — until green. VaultInventoryTests.cs, the csproj, and everything under docs/ are protected; don't touch them.

research.interface: Vault HTTP API v1 — LIST verb / ?list=true, KV v2 metadata listing, and sys/namespaces (stable)

official_sources.title: HTTP API | Vault | HashiCorp Developer
official_sources.used_for: /v1 base path, X-Vault-Token header, the LIST HTTP verb and its documented GET ?list=true equivalent, the 404 + {"errors":[]} empty-list convention, the {"errors":[...]} error document, and the 404 status doubling as invalid-path-or-unauthorized

official_sources.title: KV - Secrets Engines - HTTP API | Vault | HashiCorp Developer
official_sources.used_for: LIST /:secret-mount-path/metadata/:path for enumerating a KV v2 tree, the data.keys response shape, and the trailing-slash convention marking folders vs leaf secrets

official_sources.title: /sys/namespaces - HTTP API | Vault | HashiCorp Developer
official_sources.used_for: LIST /v1/sys/namespaces (Enterprise) response shape: data.keys plus data.key_info with per-namespace id/path/custom_metadata

official_sources.title: Vault Enterprise namespaces | Vault | HashiCorp Developer
official_sources.used_for: namespace targeting semantics: X-Vault-Namespace holds an absolute or relative namespace path, request paths are resolved relative to it (header ns1/ns2/ + path secret/foo = ns1/ns2/secret/foo), and namespaces nest hierarchically as isolated mini-Vaults

research.language_rationale: HashiCorp does not publish a first-party .NET Vault SDK (VaultSharp is community-maintained), and this harness pins zero NuGet runtime dependencies beyond the test stack; the LIST/?list=true HTTP surface is the documented interface, and HttpClient with a custom HttpMethod plus System.Text.Json is the idiomatic .NET way to speak it.
